landmarks.name: Campfire Gathering Place
landmarks.shortDescription: A place where people have gathered around fires to share stories for thousands of years.
landmarks.fullStory:
For thousands of years, people have come together around fires like this one. Elders sat with younger people and shared stories about the land, the seasons, and the Dreamtime.

The campfire was the centre of community life. Around the fire, people talked, settled disagreements, sang songs, and danced together. The fire was seen as a gift from the ancestors, and keeping it burning meant keeping culture alive.

Today, campfire gatherings are still important to Aboriginal communities. They are places for healing, learning, and coming together as a family and community.

landmarks.name: Sacred Waterhole
landmarks.shortDescription: A waterhole that holds deep meaning for the people who have cared for it since ancient times.
landmarks.fullStory:
This waterhole is important to the Traditional Owners who have looked after this land for many generations. In Dreamtime stories of the Kulin Nation, waterholes were made by the Rainbow Serpent as it moved across the land and shaped the rivers and creeks.

Waterholes were not just places to get water. They were meeting spots, spiritual places, and markers of the journeys of the ancestors.

Aboriginal people knew which waterholes held water all year and which ones dried up in summer. This knowledge was passed down carefully through stories and teaching.

landmarks.name: Rock Art Gallery
landmarks.shortDescription: Paintings made on rock, telling stories that have lasted for thousands of years.
landmarks.fullStory:
The rock art in Victoria is some of the oldest art in the world. Paintings in the Grampians (Gariwerd) are over 20,000 years old.

These paintings tell Dreamtime stories, mark important places, and record history. The artists used natural paints made from ochre, a type of coloured rock, mixed with water or animal fat.

You can see hand stencils, animal tracks, and symbols in these paintings. Some places have layers of art painted over thousands of years.

These sites are sacred and are still important places for Aboriginal people today.

landmarks.name: Corroboree Ground
landmarks.shortDescription: A place where people come together to dance, sing, and share stories through ceremony.
landmarks.fullStory:
This is a corroboree ground, a place where Aboriginal communities come together for ceremony. Corroborees can bring hundreds of people from different clan groups, with some travelling very far to be there.

At a corroboree, people dance and sing stories from the Dreamtime. They paint their bodies with ochre and wear special clothes. Each dance tells a story about an ancestor, an animal, or the land.

Corroborees are also a chance for groups to trade, to strengthen friendships, and for young people to learn the culture. The Kulin Nation held large gatherings at sites across Victoria, where different language groups came together.

landmarks.name: Bush Tucker Trail
landmarks.shortDescription: Native plants and foods that Aboriginal people have known and used for thousands of years.
landmarks.fullStory:
Aboriginal people have known how to find food in this land for over 65,000 years. Victoria's forests and plains were full of food for those who knew where to look.

Some important foods from this area:

Murnong (yam daisy) has yellow flowers and roots that can be cooked and eaten. It used to grow across the plains in huge numbers.

Kangaroo grass seeds were collected and ground into flour for making bread.

Wattle seeds are full of protein and were used in cooking and drinks.

Looking after the land was part of getting food. Aboriginal people used careful burning to keep the land healthy, help plants grow back, and create good homes for animals.

landmarks.name: Songline Path
landmarks.shortDescription: An ancient route through the land, remembered through songs and stories.
landmarks.fullStory:
Songlines, also called Dreaming tracks, are paths across the land that are recorded in songs, stories, and dances.

Each songline tells the story of an ancestor who walked the land and shaped it during the Dreamtime. By singing the right songs in the right order, a person can find their way across hundreds of kilometres, even through land they have never visited before.

Songlines also hold important knowledge: where to find water and food, which places are sacred, and which groups are responsible for caring for which parts of the land.

In Victoria, songlines connected the Kulin Nation to communities far away. These paths were used to trade goods like stone axes, possum skin cloaks, and ochre, and to share songs, dances, and stories.

Songlines are still alive and important to Aboriginal communities today.
